column2: — Good morning!
— she said while still in the doorway.
Then she came up to Ivan, he rose, and they embraced.
— Hello, Ivan.
— How are things?
— Ivan straightened his back a little.
He wore a shirt tucked into semi-formal trousers, and he wore glasses.
— With me?
Fine.
I see you never went back to your wild youth.
So serious, — said Lilichka and looked at Lusya.
The little girl was asleep, nestled against Miyechka's chest.
— What a little angel, — Lilichka said quietly.
— Doesn't look like you at all.
— She does, she does, — the grandmother cut in.
— Look, the nose is exactly like Ivan's!
She and Marta exchanged glances.
— Lilya, why don't you show me the garden, — said Ivan.
She got up, and the two of them walked along the path between the pumpkins.
— I have a request… — Ivan began.
— Go on.
— Let Lusya stay with you for a couple of weeks.
— What's happened?
— asked Lilichka.
— Look, here's where we're growing pumpkins.
— Natalya… She's ill.
— Something serious?
— Serious.
— And what's the prognosis?
— I don't know, Lilya, what the prognosis is, but it's better not to give her the child right now.
— There's no one else?
— asked Lilichka.
— No one, — Ivan shook his head.
— The reason I'm asking.
It's best if the child knows the people she's left with, you understand?
— I know…
— And what does Natalya say?
— Shall I quote her?
— Quote.
— Natalya said: 'Out of your whole screwed-up little family the only normal one is your ex-wife.'
Lilichka burst out laughing.
— Sorry, — she said, stifling her laughter.
— It's not funny, it's just really true, forgive me.
— That's why I brought you the child, Lilya, — said Ivan.
— Everything will be fine, Ivan, — Lilichka spoke lightly.
Nothing could interrupt her positivity.
Now she enchanted everyone with it, now she infuriated them, but it was an inseparable part of her, like an arm or a leg.
And so Lusya, and a bag of Lusya's dresses, began to live with them.
Marta went to bed early; she was nauseous again, and cramps seized her legs.
Behind Marta's back the grandmother said it was because she was forty, and carrying children at forty is not the same as at twenty.
As she said it she looked at Miyechka, and Miyechka was thinking about her imaginary girl, who had suddenly materialized.

Lusya fell asleep beside the grandmother — all day she had hauled the old cat around and brought him dandelions, and in the evening she suddenly felt sorry for herself, felt sad, mournful and lonely, as everyone sometimes does when the sun goes down.
The little girl began to cry, and then the grandmother drew her close and began to tell about the adventures of little Marusenka — a small girl whom fire helicopters were searching for in the forest.

The valiant firemen had not yet managed to determine Marusenka's location, had not yet managed to set the tall fire ladder against the fir tree where she sat, when Lusya was already asleep, nestled against the grandmother's warm side.
The grandmother was so small that the two-year-old child was half her height.

Then Lilichka and Miyechka went outside, where dusk had already enveloped the pear trees, which no one liked or ate, and the birch in the distance was almost no longer visible, you could only hear it rustling its leaves.
They sat down on their favorite steps on the porch, from which the concrete had already begun to crumble a little, and threw their grandfather's one jacket over the two of them.
— So what are you going to do?
— asked Lilichka.
— Meaning?
— said Miyechka, though she knew perfectly well what her sister was asking about.
— I don't know what I'll do.
— That's why I'm asking.
Where did you two meet?
— Oh, it doesn't matter.
I don't even really remember, it was just like that — and there he was.
Who am I telling this to?
You know it better than I do.
— I'm asking precisely because I thought this could have happened to anyone, but not to you.
— Things didn't turn out as expected, — sighed Miyechka, picking at the concrete on the steps.
— I'm in a trap that I set for myself.
— Wait, I'll bring some sweets, — said Lilichka.

She jumped up, flashing her bare legs, threw off the jacket and bounded off to rummage under the stairs up in the attic, where she hid sweets from who knows whom: she simply needed to have a stash that no one but her would touch.
— So what about the silence?
— Lilichka came back, already chewing a sweet.
— It's as if I'm never entirely sure of anything.
— Which one will you have?
'Starlight' or 'Southern Night'?
— You even have to ask?
— Got it.
Miyechka unwrapped the top of the sweet and began nibbling at its tip.
— He's a coarse, silent type, Lilichka.
I've sunk to the point of asking him, 'do you love me?'
Just imagine.
— And he?
— He said nothing.
— What a jerk.
— It's as if I'm blind, walking through a dark forest and falling all the time.
And he walks ahead, without turning around, without offering his hand, you understand?
— What on earth did you find in him?
— Lilichka spoke with a sweet in her mouth.
— He's wonderful.
Wonderful, unclear, and that other one, distant, with white hands and shiny nails — clear, calm, though not my own.
'Mom said,' Miyechka thought, 'to choose the one who loves more, so that this love lasts longer.'
But what about Mom?
She was always the more loved one, she quickly gave birth to children, and then she could live as she pleased, leaving the indifferent, enlightened father among his tulips.
He never even told her that he was against her going off anywhere.
He nodded, agreed.
I should have given birth at twenty and now have nothing to worry about, and live entirely on my own, with no one, just looking after the children in turn.'
Maria, for her part, feeling complete freedom now that her daughters were grown, gladly moved into a small apartment with a railing-grated balcony.

It was in this very apartment that the grandmother had once grown up; here her parents had lived their whole lives, parents who wanted a better fate for their daughter than the post of housekeeper in an enormous house.
Maria now seemed to be playing the role the grandmother had once left unfinished.
Teaching courses, traveling, an apartment, grilles, flowers.
Sometimes she tried to persuade the grandmother to move in with her, but the grandmother wouldn't, making use of the services of carers instead.
'You don't have to look after me,' she said to Maria, 'tend to your flowers.'
As long as I can walk, I'll guard these bushes.
Only once I die, don't you go poking into those bushes, you don't need them.'

Sometimes the two of them would be in the city after all and would sit on that little grated balcony, beneath which people walked, and across the way there was a café with an enormous doughnut in the window.
'On a balcony like this my mother's aunt died,' the grandmother would then say.
— And do you remember Violetta Petrivna?
Well, her daughter crippled herself just like that.'
The grandmother recalled this every time on this balcony, onto which her mother had forbidden her to go when she was little.
In the morning Miyechka set Lusya on her hip and went to the shop.
It was sweltering in the shop, a tired fly buzzed about, sometimes landing on the ring marks left by beer glasses.
Lusya pointed at the fly with her finger and smiled.
In the shop Lusya wheedled (simply by pointing a finger) a lollipop on a stick, and Miyechka bought the cornmeal she had planned to.
She was going to make mamalyga, which they would spread onto the large pieces of meat already thawing in the kitchen.

Miyechka walked back and thought that God, or evolution, had for some reason invented the waist for a woman, so that men's hands could hold it or so that she could carry her little children on it, because children are cozy and comfortable on a hip.
Lusya sat calmly, licking her sweet, and didn't say a single word.
At lunch, when the mamalyga had almost all been eaten, the grandmother said:
— Girls, is it normal that our Lusya doesn't say anything?
Everyone shrugged, Lusya was licking a tomato.
— Didn't Ivan tell you anything?
— Miyechka asked Lilya.
Lilichka, as the person closest to the little girl, since Lusya's father had made a child with her too, simply replied:
— I didn't ask.
— And hasn't he called you?
— asked the grandmother.
— I haven't looked.
— Maybe he's curious how his child is getting on here?
— Marta cut in.
— Maybe he is, — said Lilichka, looking somewhere inside herself, and then added: — But why did he make such a little girl with her, and not with me?
Lusya meanwhile had climbed down from Miyechka's lap and was picking dandelions.
Her fair, perfectly straight and thin hair barely reached her shoulders.
The little girl was not beautiful, but something about her recalled the depictions of infantas on old canvases — large, slightly bulging eyes, a high forehead.
— Whom does this child take after?
— asked the grandmother.
— Granny, you said yourself she looks like Ivan.
— Oh, come now!
— the grandmother waved her hand.
— The things you say.
Lilichka got up from the table and went into the house.
And what's more, she didn't bound along as always, but walked at a measured pace.
— Lilichka wants a little daughter, — said the grandmother.
— Everyone wants a little daughter, — retorted Miyechka.
— But everyone gets given sons, — Marta spoke up, kneading her own arm to ease the cramps.
— Now could you imagine Lilichka getting back together with Ivan?
— asked the grandmother.
— Oh, Gra-a-anny… — Miyechka groaned.
— You told me the same thing about that what's-his-name too, and look how it turned out?
In her youth Lilichka had a couple of times gotten together with a strange, handsome Canadian, with whom it's not even known whether she so much as kissed.
He was such an unearthly creature that who knows whether he even felt any attraction to women.
And Lilichka was merciful.
The next morning Miyechka woke up and said:
— The pumpkins will be putting out blossoms soon, and here we are still driving on winter tires.
She nodded toward Lilichka.
The latter stood leaning her back against the kitchen wall with a mug in her hands, nodded back to her sister and disappeared into the corridor.
She came out with the car keys on her finger.
Then the two of them climbed down into the garage pit and tried to get the summer tires out of it.
The grandmother came out of the house and suggested phoning someone, so that somebody would come and help.
Lilichka shouted from the pit that there was no need.
Miyechka was already rolling a tire.
She stopped by the trunk, looked at the grandmother — who stood by the lilac bush with its faded, yellowed blossoms and was very displeased with what was going on.
— So this is how it is, girls, driving around without men.
— Oh, Granny, — sighed Miyechka, holding her foot on the tire.
— Besides, — Lilichka peeked out of the pit, — along with the right to vote they gave us the right to haul tires.
— We've had the right to haul tires for a long time, — retorted the grandmother.
— Do you know how many tons of coal I unloaded while your grandfather was building a state that then collapsed with a crash and nearly crushed beneath it those it hadn't crushed earlier?
The grandmother was so small against the backdrop of the enormous trees that it seemed she had carried those tons of coal two lumps per trip — one in each hand.
Miyechka waited for her sister to roll the last tire out of the pit, so they could load them into the car together.
— Girls, take them like this, like this, — the grandmother showed with her hands how to pick up the tires, as if she had spent her whole life doing nothing but rolling them out of the pit.
In reality she wasn't even sure where they screwed on.
Lusya came out of the house.
She was barefoot, sleepy, bewildered, sucking on her own little nightgown and looking for someone.
Marta came out after her, holding her back.
Marta had dark circles under her eyes.
She had slept badly.
It would be more accurate to say that she had already forgotten when she'd last slept well.
Lusya's chin began to tremble ever so slightly: the little girl didn't know whom to go to.
Lilichka, brushing off her hands after the dirty tire, looked at the child somehow pensively, as if from afar, as though she were sitting somewhere in a park watching passersby.
— Lilya, — Marta spoke up.
Despite the dark circles and the pregnancy, her voice was stern, and Lilya started, went up to Lusya and took her in her arms, trying not to touch the child with her palms.
Lusya burst into tears.
— Poor child, — said the grandmother.
Miyechka came up to the grandmother and whispered:
— Don't say things like that.
After that no one pitied Lusya.
Marta carried outside the bag of Lusya's clothes that Ivan had left.
They had already taken a few dresses out of it, but hadn't gone through the clothes completely.
Having tipped everything out onto the morning grass, from which the dew had only recently lifted, they all began to look it over.
